what_it_does: The CSS realisation of the motion axis values — defines each .mo-* class and its @keyframes (breathe, pulse, bob, tilt, spin, float, glow), so applying a class returned by CV_AXES.css('motion', value) animates the element. It is the single home for HOW each motion looks, complementing motion-axis.js which owns WHICH motions exist.
observations: Header states the split: the axis JS is the source of which motions exist, this CSS is the source of how each looks; consumers apply CV_AXES.css('motion', value).
[class*="mo-"] sets transform-origin:center on any motion-carrying element.
Looping animations (breathe, pulse, bob, tilt, spin, float, glow) are wrapped in @media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference) so reduced-motion users get the settled end-state, never a loop.
Durations vary per motion (breathe 3.6s, pulse 1.8s, bob 2.4s, tilt 4s, spin 2.2s, float 4.2s, glow 2.6s); easing uses var(--ease-soft, ease-in-out) with spin using linear.
mo-tilt:hover is deliberately kept OUTSIDE the reduced-motion guard (comment: user-initiated, brief), pausing the loop and applying a rotate+scale transition.
glow keyframe uses color-mix(in srgb, var(--accent-gold) …) for a drop-shadow halo.
Keyframe names are cvmo-* prefixed; class names mo-* — matching the css fields in motion-axis.js.
standouts: Reduced-motion accessibility built in: loops gated, settled end-state default, hover-intent kept as a brief user-initiated exception.
Easing/durations reference tokens (var(--ease-soft), var(--accent-gold)) rather than hardcoded values where it matters.
Explicit articulation that this file is the 'HOW' home and the axis JS is the 'WHICH' home.
conventions: Single-home discipline: animation definitions live here once; the axis JS references class names.
References design tokens (--ease-soft, --accent-gold) with fallbacks rather than raw values.
Accessibility-first prefers-reduced-motion gating, per tokens/motion.css 'nothing teleports'.
cvmo-/mo- naming consistent with the axis value css fields.
notes: The animation home for the motion axis — each mo-* class + cvmo-* keyframe realises one axis value, all loops gated behind prefers-reduced-motion with hover-intent (tilt) as the brief exception, easing/colour drawn from tokens.
questions: The durations (3.6s, 1.8s, etc.) are hardcoded here rather than token-referenced — is that intentional (per-motion tuning) or a candidate for tokenisation under the no-raw-values rule?
purpose_vs_actual: Match — the header claims this is the single CSS home realising each motion axis value, and it defines exactly the .mo-* classes and matching keyframes, with the reduced-motion discipline it promises.
apparent_themes: Single source of truth (animation defined once, referenced by class).
Token-referenced styling over hardcoded values for easing/colour.
intention_signals: To give the motion axis a single, accessible CSS home where each named motion is implemented once with token-aligned easing/colour and reduced-motion safety, so any consumer animates by applying a class without owning the animation.
novelty: Pairing each axis value with a keyframe in a dedicated 'how' file, and treating hover-intent motion as a deliberate exception to the reduced-motion guard.
contribution: Contributes the concrete animation implementations for the motion axis — the keyframes, durations, easing, reduced-motion gating, and hover-intent handling that realise each motion value.
summary_for_embedding: A CSS file defining the .mo-* classes and cvmo-* @keyframes (breathe/pulse/bob/tilt/spin/float/glow) that realise the motion axis values, gated behind prefers-reduced-motion with token-referenced easing.
intention_for_embedding: To be the single CSS home for how each motion axis value looks, implementing each animation once with accessibility and token alignment.
